}

issue.title: `cabs (x + 0i)`and `cabs(0.0 + xi)` should be optimized to `abs(x)` even without `-ffast-math`
issue.body:
Take the fortran testcase:
```
subroutine foo(a,b,n)
  real(kind(1d0))::a
  real(kind(1d0))::b
  integer::i,n
  complex(kind(1d0))::c

  c = a
     b=abs(c)

end subroutine foo
```

This should be just optimized to `abs(a)` rather than a call to `cabs`.

C testcase:
```
double
foo (double e)
{
  _Complex double x = __builtin_complex(0.0,e);
  return __builtin_cabs(e);
}
double
foo1 (double e)
{
  return __builtin_cabs(e);
}
```

Likewise for `hypot` too.
```
double
fooh (double e)
{
  return __builtin_hypot(e, 0.0);
}
```

The definition of hypot in C99 allows for this.

GCC has been handling `foo1`  function since before GCC 3.4.0. `fooh` function since GCC 4.4.0.
This is more likely to show up with fortran code than C or C++ code.
issue.author: pinskia

comments.author: dtcxzyw
Mehdibenhadjkhelifa
comments.body: hello @HendrikHuebner . Are you still working on this?

comments.author: HendrikHuebner
comments.body: yes. I would still like to work on this

comments.author: akshaykumars614
comments.body:
@pinskia, can you help me understand this issue please? I am new here. This should be optimized even without any optimizations specified?

If yes, the, should there be a cmp inst to check if any of them is zero or we do it internally and just generate the cabs(x) ?

comments.author: HendrikHuebner
comments.body: @akshaykumars614 the libcall simplify pass optimizes calls to libc functions before generating IR. It's like simplifying a mathematical formula. In this case, if the real or imaginary part of the input is zero, we can just replace the call to cabs with a call to abs. But please do not work on this. I'm about to submit a PR.

comments.author: akshaykumars614
comments.body: Ohh okay! I'm not working on it. I'm new here and trying to understand the issue. Thanks for the explanation.

comments.author: HendrikHuebner
comments.body: you can have a look through the code in SimplifyLibCalls.cpp. It's very interesting imo.
